title: Shock: Cardiogenic & Distributive
targetAudience: Medical Students, Residents, Critical Care Fellows, Emergency Medicine, Cardiology
objectives: Classify shock by mechanism (distributive, cardiogenic, hypovolemic, obstructive)
Recognize clinical features and hemodynamic profiles of each shock type
Apply SCAI classification for cardiogenic shock staging
Select appropriate vasopressors and inotropes based on shock type
Manage septic shock per Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines
Understand mixed shock states and their management challenges

guidelineReferences.name: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Evans L, et al. Intensive Care Med. 2021;47:1181-1247
keyPoints: Norepinephrine first-line vasopressor for septic shock
Dobutamine for persistent hypoperfusion despite fluids

guidelineReferences.name: ACC Expert Consensus on Cardiogenic Shock 2025
ISHLT Consensus on HF-related Cardiogenic Shock 2024

vignette.text:
A 68-year-old man presents with chest pain, diaphoresis, and progressive shortness of breath for 3 hours. He becomes increasingly confused and his skin is cool and mottled.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, Type 2 diabetes, Hyperlipidemia, Prior MI 5 years ago

Medications: Aspirin, metoprolol, atorvastatin, lisinopril, metformin

Allergies: NKDA

physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, S3 gallop, JVD to angle of jaw, cool extremities, weak pulses
physicalExam.extremities: Cool, mottled, delayed capillary refill >4 seconds

diagnostics.ECG: ST elevations in V1-V4 with reciprocal changes in inferior leads - STEMI
diagnostics.Echo_bedside: Severe LV dysfunction, EF 15-20%, anterior wall akinesis, moderate MR

questions.stem: This patient is hypotensive with elevated lactate and signs of poor perfusion.
questions.question: What are the four major types of shock?

options.text: All shock is the same pathophysiology

DISTRIBUTIVE: Vasodilation, low SVR (septic, anaphylactic, neurogenic); CARDIOGENIC: Pump failure, low CO, high filling pressures; HYPOVOLEMIC: Volume loss, low preload (hemorrhage, dehydration); OBSTRUCTIVE: Mechanical obstruction to flow (PE, tamponade, tension pneumo); Each has distinct hemodynamic profile and treatment

questions.explanation: Four shock categories: (1) DISTRIBUTIVE: Inappropriate vasodilation → low SVR, high or normal CO initially. Subtypes: Septic (most common), anaphylactic, neurogenic. Warm extremities early; (2) CARDIOGENIC: Pump failure → low CO, high filling pressures, high SVR. Causes: MI (this patient), HF, arrhythmia, valvular. Cold, clammy, pulmonary edema; (3) HYPOVOLEMIC: Volume loss → low preload, low CO. Causes: Hemorrhage, dehydration, burns. Tachycardia, flat JVP; (4) OBSTRUCTIVE: Mechanical obstruction → impaired filling or output. Causes: PE, tamponade, tension pneumothorax. Often with specific findings (pulsus paradoxus, unilateral breath sounds). This patient: CARDIOGENIC (STEMI with low EF, pulmonary edema, elevated JVP).

questions.question: What are the hemodynamic profiles of cardiogenic vs distributive shock?

options.text: Septic shock never affects the heart

CARDIOGENIC: Low CO, high SVR (compensatory vasoconstriction), high filling pressures (PCWP elevated), cold extremities; DISTRIBUTIVE (septic): Initially high CO, low SVR (vasodilation), low/normal filling pressures, warm extremities; Late septic shock may have LOW CO (septic cardiomyopathy); MIXED SHOCK: Common - features of both
All shock has the same hemodynamics

questions.explanation: Hemodynamic profiles: CARDIOGENIC: Cardiac Index <2.2 L/min/m², SVR elevated (>1200 dyne·s/cm⁵), PCWP elevated (>18 mmHg), SvO2 low (<65%), Cold extremities (vasoconstriction). DISTRIBUTIVE (Septic): Early: CI elevated (hyperdynamic), SVR low (<800), PCWP normal/low, SvO2 elevated (>70% - inadequate extraction), Warm extremities (vasodilation). Late: CI may fall (septic cardiomyopathy - up to 50% of patients). MIXED SHOCK: Very common in critically ill - cardiogenic + distributive features. Advanced cardiogenic → bacterial translocation → inflammatory response. Septic → cardiomyopathy. Treatment becomes complex.

whyChain.answer: Treatment differs dramatically: Cardiogenic shock needs inotropes and afterload reduction (when tolerated), fluids may worsen pulmonary edema. Distributive shock needs fluids and vasopressors to restore SVR. Wrong treatment can be fatal - fluids in cardiogenic shock worsen pulmonary edema; vasoconstrictors alone in cardiogenic shock increase afterload without improving output.

questions.stem: This patient has cardiogenic shock from STEMI.
questions.question: How is cardiogenic shock staged using the SCAI classification?

options.text: All cardiogenic shock is the same severity

SCAI Stages: A (At Risk) - risk factors, no hypoperfusion; B (Beginning) - hypotension OR hypoperfusion, not both; C (Classic) - hypotension AND hypoperfusion requiring intervention; D (Deteriorating) - failing initial therapy, escalating support; E (Extremis) - refractory shock, near death, CPR/ECMO. Higher stages = higher mortality

questions.explanation: SCAI Cardiogenic Shock Staging: Stage A (At Risk): Large MI, prior HF, no current signs; Stage B (Beginning): Hypotension (SBP <90 or MAP <60) OR signs of hypoperfusion (elevated lactate, oliguria), but not both; Stage C (Classic): Hypotension AND hypoperfusion, requires vasopressors/inotropes or MCS - this is 'classic' cardiogenic shock; Stage D (Deteriorating): Failing initial therapy, escalating vasopressors/inotropes, worsening labs; Stage E (Extremis): Refractory shock, cardiac arrest, CPR, ECMO as bridge. This patient: SCAI Stage C (hypotension + hypoperfusion + elevated lactate). Mortality increases with stage: B ~10%, C ~30%, D ~50%, E ~70%+.

questions.stem: You need to stabilize this patient with cardiogenic shock from STEMI.
questions.question: What is the treatment approach for cardiogenic shock?

options.text: Priorities: (1) REVASCULARIZATION for ACS-related CS - emergent PCI within 2 hours; (2) Hemodynamic support while awaiting: Inopressors (norepinephrine + dobutamine) or (epinephrine) for BP + inotropy; Avoid excessive fluids if pulmonary edema; (3) Mechanical circulatory support (MCS) if refractory: IABP, Impella, ECMO; (4) Treat congestion if present; (5) Minimize vasopressor duration/dose

questions.explanation: Cardiogenic shock treatment: (1) REVASCULARIZATION: For ACS-related CS, emergent coronary angiography and PCI within 2 hours - this is definitive treatment; (2) HEMODYNAMIC SUPPORT: Inopressor (norepinephrine preferred over dopamine) for MAP, Add inotrope (dobutamine or milrinone) for low output, OR epinephrine monotherapy (provides both); (3) FLUIDS: Cautious - small bolus (250 mL) if no pulmonary edema, avoid if congested; (4) MECHANICAL CIRCULATORY SUPPORT (MCS): IABP (modest support), Impella (more support), VA-ECMO (most support, for extremis). Match MCS to shock severity; (5) DIURETICS: If congested (this patient has pulmonary edema); (6) Goal: Minimize vasoactive drug dose and duration - associated with worse outcomes.

whyChain.answer: SOAP II trial: Dopamine associated with more arrhythmias and higher mortality in cardiogenic shock subgroup compared to norepinephrine. Current guidelines recommend norepinephrine as first-line vasopressor if needed. Dobutamine or milrinone added for inotropic support. Epinephrine is an alternative single agent.

questions.stem: You need to choose appropriate vasoactive agents.
questions.question: What are the key vasopressors and inotropes and their mechanisms?

options.text: All vasopressors work the same way

NOREPINEPHRINE: α1 >> β1, increases SVR and MAP, mild inotropy - first-line for septic shock; EPINEPHRINE: α1 + β1 + β2, increases SVR, CO, HR - useful inopressor; VASOPRESSIN: V1 receptor, increases SVR, no cardiac effect - adjunct in septic shock; DOBUTAMINE: β1 >> β2, increases CO, decreases SVR (inodilator) - for low output; DOPAMINE: Dose-dependent α/β/DA - more arrhythmias, generally avoided

questions.explanation: Vasopressor/inotrope mechanisms: NOREPINEPHRINE: Potent α1, moderate β1. Increases SVR (primary), mild inotropy. First-line for septic shock (SSC guidelines); EPINEPHRINE: Strong α1, β1, β2. Increases SVR, CO, HR, glucose. Useful as single inopressor agent; VASOPRESSIN: V1 receptors (non-adrenergic). Increases SVR. Useful adjunct in catecholamine-resistant shock, doesn't increase myocardial O2 demand; PHENYLEPHRINE: Pure α1. Increases SVR only. Useful for pure vasodilation (neurogenic shock, anesthesia); DOBUTAMINE: Strong β1, moderate β2. Increases CO, may decrease SVR (inodilator). For low output states; MILRINONE: PDE3 inhibitor. Increases CO, decreases SVR. Similar to dobutamine; DOPAMINE: Dose-dependent effects. More arrhythmias than norepinephrine. Generally avoided as first-line.

questions.stem: A different patient has septic shock from pneumonia.
questions.question: What is the management of septic shock per Surviving Sepsis Campaign?

options.text: Hour-1 Bundle: Measure lactate, blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics WITHIN 1 HOUR, IV crystalloid 30 mL/kg for hypotension or lactate ≥4; Vasopressors: NOREPINEPHRINE first-line (target MAP ≥65 mmHg), Add VASOPRESSIN if norepinephrine insufficient, Add DOBUTAMINE if persistent hypoperfusion despite fluids/pressors; Source control; Consider corticosteroids if refractory

questions.explanation: Septic Shock Management (SSC 2021): HOUR-1 BUNDLE: (1) Measure lactate (repeat if elevated); (2) Blood cultures before antibiotics (but don't delay); (3) Broad-spectrum ANTIBIOTICS within 1 HOUR; (4) IV crystalloid 30 mL/kg for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L; (5) Vasopressors if hypotensive during/after fluids. VASOPRESSORS: Norepinephrine FIRST-LINE (target MAP ≥65 mmHg), Add vasopressin (up to 0.03 U/min) as second-line, Add epinephrine if inadequate MAP. INOTROPES: Dobutamine for persistent hypoperfusion with adequate volume status and MAP. SOURCE CONTROL: Identify and treat infection source. CORTICOSTEROIDS: Consider hydrocortisone 200 mg/day if vasopressor-refractory shock.

questions.stem: The septic shock patient remains hypotensive on norepinephrine 0.5 mcg/kg/min.
questions.question: What is the approach to refractory septic shock?

options.text: Add VASOPRESSIN (0.03-0.04 U/min) to norepinephrine - different receptor, may reduce catecholamine dose; Consider EPINEPHRINE if still refractory; HYDROCORTISONE 200 mg/day for vasopressor-refractory shock (may be given as 50 mg q6h); Reassess volume status, source control, antimicrobial coverage; Consider septic cardiomyopathy - add dobutamine if CO low

questions.explanation: Refractory septic shock: (1) ADD VASOPRESSIN: 0.03-0.04 U/min (fixed dose, not titrated). Different receptor (V1) from catecholamines - may work when α-receptors saturated. May allow catecholamine dose reduction (less arrhythmias); (2) EPINEPHRINE: As third-line or alternative; (3) CORTICOSTEROIDS: Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day (50 mg q6h or 200 mg continuous) if vasopressor-refractory. Improves shock reversal, questionable mortality benefit; (4) REASSESS: Volume status (may need more fluids or diuresis if congested), Source control (undrained abscess?), Antimicrobial coverage (resistant organism?); (5) SEPTIC CARDIOMYOPATHY: Up to 50% develop. If CO low despite fluids/pressors, add dobutamine or consider echo/PA catheter.

questions.stem: A patient with decompensated heart failure develops sepsis.
questions.question: How is mixed cardiogenic-distributive shock managed?

options.text: Treat as pure septic shock with large volume fluids

CHALLENGES: Fluid resuscitation (sepsis) may worsen pulmonary edema (cardiogenic); APPROACH: Cautious fluids - small boluses with reassessment; Norepinephrine for vasodilation; Inotrope (dobutamine) if cardiac output remains low; Invasive hemodynamic monitoring often needed (PA catheter or equivalent); Treat both components - antibiotics AND cardiac optimization; Consider MCS in severe cases

questions.explanation: Mixed cardiogenic-distributive shock: COMMON SCENARIOS: HF patient with sepsis, Post-cardiac surgery vasoplegia, Late cardiogenic shock with bacterial translocation, Septic cardiomyopathy in septic shock. CHALLENGES: Standard sepsis fluids (30 mL/kg) can be devastating in cardiogenic shock. APPROACH: (1) Cautious fluids - 250 mL boluses with frequent reassessment (lung exam, echo, CVP/PCWP); (2) Norepinephrine for vasodilation component; (3) Inotrope (dobutamine) if CO remains low after volume optimization; (4) Invasive monitoring often necessary - PA catheter provides CO, SVR, filling pressures to guide therapy; (5) Source control and antibiotics for infection; (6) MCS may be needed if refractory.

questions.stem: Returning to the original patient with cardiogenic shock from STEMI.
questions.question: What is the complete management approach?

options.text: Immediate: (1) Activate cath lab for emergent PCI (definitive treatment); (2) Hemodynamic support: Start NOREPINEPHRINE for MAP, Add DOBUTAMINE for low CO (or epinephrine alone); (3) Avoid large fluid boluses (pulmonary edema present); (4) Aspirin, heparin, P2Y12 inhibitor per STEMI protocol; (5) Consider IABP or Impella if deteriorating; (6) Intubate if respiratory failure; (7) ICU with continuous monitoring; Goal: Emergent revascularization + hemodynamic support
Wait for cardiac enzymes before intervention

questions.explanation: Complete cardiogenic shock (STEMI) management: IMMEDIATE: (1) ACTIVATE CATH LAB - emergent PCI is definitive treatment for AMI-CS, target <2 hours; (2) HEMODYNAMIC SUPPORT: Norepinephrine for MAP (target ≥65 mmHg), Add dobutamine for low cardiac output, OR epinephrine as single inopressor agent; (3) AVOID excessive fluids - this patient has pulmonary edema; (4) STEMI PROTOCOL: Aspirin 325 mg, P2Y12 inhibitor, Anticoagulation (heparin); (5) MECHANICAL SUPPORT: Consider IABP or Impella if deteriorating, VA-ECMO if extremis; (6) RESPIRATORY: High-flow O2, NIPPV, or intubation if needed; (7) ICU admission, continuous hemodynamic monitoring, arterial line; (8) DIURETICS: After stabilization for pulmonary edema. This patient is SCAI Stage C - needs aggressive treatment and revascularization.
